title: Dynamic Competition in Negotiated Price Markets
abstract: Using contract-level data for the Canadian mortgage market, this paper provides evidence of an “invest-and-harvest” pricing pattern. We build a dynamic model of price negotiation with search and switching frictions to capture key market features. We estimate the model and use it to investigate the effects of market frictions and the resulting dynamic competition on borrowers' and banks' payoffs. We show that dynamic pricing and the presence of search and switching costs have important implications for public policies.

article.introduction:
Many markets feature repeated interactionswhere customers and firms negotiate over prices. Examples include the market for mortgages (Woodward and Hall (2012), Allen, Clark, and Houde (2019)), unsecured consumer credit (Cuesta and Sepulveda (2021), Agarwal et al. (2023b)), small business lending (Degryse and Ongena (2005)), auto insurance (Honka (2014)), health insurance (Dafny (2010)), and many business-to-business transactions (Marshall (2020), Salz (2022)). Customers in these markets normally face nontrivial costs of searching for price quotes and switching providers. Both frictions lead to a form of lock-in that places incumbent firms in a stronger bargaining position than rival firms and thereby increases their market power. Recognizing the additional rents associated with being an incumbent, forward-looking firms compete more aggressively ex ante to build their customer base. In these markets, we should observe a dynamic pricing pattern: firms use relatively low prices to attract new customers and poach those of their rivals, and then charge higher prices once customers are locked in.1
The main contribution of this paper is to introduce a framework for studying firms' dynamic pricing strategies in markets featuring price negotiation, repeated interaction, and search/switching frictions. We focus on the Canadian mortgage market. In Canada, a typical newly originated mortgage amortizes in 25 years. The majority of home buyers take out five-year fixed-rate mortgages (FRMs). Hence, every five years borrowers are forced to renew their outstanding loan with either the current provider or a rival lender, and a new interest rate must be negotiated. Lenders' pricing, however, is opaque. Borrowers often have to visit several local banks to negotiate meaningful quotes. In addition to the search costs, borrowers face costs of switching lenders.2We take advantage of the deterministic timing for renegotiation to gain insight into the role of market frictions in the dynamic pricing game played by lenders.
Combining anonymized credit bureau data with contract-level administrative data, we construct a data set that contains detailed mortgage contract information, borrower characteristics, and borrowers' switching activities for a cross-section of new borrowers and first-time renewers. Our descriptive analysis provides evidence of “invest-and-harvest” pricing: borrowers who renew with their incumbent bank pay on average 4.0 basis points (bps) more than new borrowers, while borrowers who switch lenders at renewal pay on average 7.9 bps less than those who stay. Consider an average newly originated mortgage of $279,000 that amortizes in 25 years. The different rates imply differences in interest costs over five years of $526 to $1,043. In spite of these potential savings, only 9.55% of renewers switch providers. In addition, we document a game-ending effect: mortgages that are about to be paid off have higher rates than those with longer amortization as lenders have no incentive to invest at the end of the game. The observed pricing patterns motivate us to build a dynamic model of price negotiation with search and switching costs. This rich framework allows us to examine the role played by various market features, such as forward-looking firms and the coexistence of search and switching costs.
We model the mortgage financing process over the entire amortization period as a finite-period game. The first period is origination, and the subsequent periods are renewals. The game ends when the mortgage is fully paid. Each period, the borrower (new or renewer) is attached to a home bank.3The home bank moves first to offer the borrower a free initial quote. Depending on the realization of a per-bank search cost, and the expected gain from search, the borrower either accepts the offer or chooses to gather more quotes. If the borrower decides to search, she obtains quotes from an endogenously chosen set of lenders and uses the best offer in hand to negotiate for even better quotes. Lenders face heterogeneous cost realizations. They are willing to price lower than the lending costs as long as the quotes generate positive expected profits over the life of the mortgage. Importantly, lenders are forward-looking and understand the future value of being a home bank, which includes (i) the first-mover advantage of making an initial offer that could retain borrowers drawing high search costs, (ii) the opportunity to make additional offers given that borrowers always include their home bank in their choice set, and (iii) the switching costs that could prevent borrowers from leaving for rival lenders even if rivals offer slightly better quotes.
Our model describes the data-generating process in a tractable way. The model primitives are (i) borrowers' search cost distribution and switching cost and (ii) banks' lending cost distribution and discount factor. Overall, we find that banks' lending costs for a given borrower are not very dispersed. Borrowers, on the other hand, have nontrivial search and switching costs. On average, they face a per-bank search cost of $386 (i.e., 1.3% of the average interest cost) and obtain only three quotes. The estimated average switching costs are $462 and $829 for new borrowers and first-time renewers, respectively. Finally, lenders are forward-looking with an annual discount factor of 0.93, and due to the anticipated incumbency advantages created by market frictions, they have an average investment incentive of $843 per borrower. Importantly, the data rejects the null hypothesis of myopic lenders as well as restricted models in which only one type of market friction exists.
Decomposing the effect of market frictions on borrowers' total cost of mortgage financing (interest plus search/switching costs), we find an important interaction effect between the market frictions: removing both frictions results in savings that are 58% (30%) greater than the sum of the effects from eliminating search costs or switching costs alone at origination (renewal). In other words, each market friction is less anticompetitive when the other type of friction is present. In contrast, a static model (with myopic lenders) generates different welfare implications. It mistakenly predicts larger savings and overlooks the interaction between market frictions, precisely because it ignores changes in lenders' investment incentives.
We apply the insights of our model to study two counterfactual policies: (i) long-term contracting and (ii) ability-to-pay. We highlight the importance of accounting for pricing dynamics and the role of search and switching costs when studying actual policy changes.
Our first application focuses on long-term contracting. The Canadian market, with its balloon payments, has borrowers interacting with lenders at fixed intervals throughout the amortization period. We study the implications of changing the product offering from a five-year FRM to a 25-year FRM. Our dynamic model predicts that all players are better off with longer-term contracts, although the gains are small. Borrowers enjoy lower costs (search/switch more at origination but save on these costs throughout the amortization period) and lenders earn higher profits (especially nonincumbents). On the borrower side, the biggest winners are those with lower income, lower credit score, and larger loans, as well as older borrowers. In comparison, the static model predicts larger consumer gains at the expense of home bank profits.
Our second application focuses on a macroprudential policy implemented in Canada to restrict access to credit: an ability-to-pay stress test. Potential borrowers must prove that they can afford their mortgages at aqualifying rate—a rate set by the regulator to ensure borrowers have the ability to meet future obligations if rates were to rise. Crucially, at renewal the stress test applies only if borrowers switch lenders. This leads to a larger switching cost at renewal for some borrowers, and hence an increase in lenders' investment incentive at origination. Accordingly, we predict that the policy unintentionally intensifies competition for new buyers via lower rates, which spurs demand at a time when the regulator is trying to cool down the market.
There is a large empirical literature investigating search frictions in markets where firms post prices. See, for example, Sorensen (2001) for prescription drugs, Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004) for mutual funds, and Hong and Shum (2006) for textbooks. A typical assumption is that firms have common costs in servicing every consumer. In a negotiated-price market, however, the final price that a customer pays is individualized to reflect the heterogeneity in firm-specific servicing costs, which might be unobserved to researchers. The main challenge therefore is to disentangle the distribution of both servicing costs and search costs from the observed negotiated-price distribution.
There is also a large theoretical and empirical literature that focuses on switching costs in posted-price markets. See Klemperer (1995) and Farrell and Klemperer (2007) for overviews.4Recent empirical studies include Shum (2004) for breakfast cereal, Dubé, Hitsch, and Rossi (2009) for packaged foods, Osborne (2011) for laundry detergent, Handel (2013) for health insurance, Shcherbakov (2016) for cable television, Fleitasl (2016) for drug insurance, Illanes (2017) and Luco (2019) for pension plans, and MacKay and Remer (2022) for retail gasoline. In banking markets, there is also extensive interest in switching costs. See, for example, Ausubel (1991) for credit cards, Kim, Kliger, and Vale (2003) for bank loans, Ho (2015) and Carbo-Valverde, Hannan, and Rodriguez-Fernandez (2011) for deposits, Ioannidou and Ongena (2010) for firm lending, and Thiel (2018) for the Dutch mortgage market.
From the list above, Dubé, Hitsch, and Rossi (2009) and Shcherbakov (2016) investigate switching costs in dynamic frameworks. The authors find that switching costs can lead to lower equilibrium prices. Consistent with their findings, we show that new borrowers' interest costs are lower with switching costs than without. As a result, policies aimed at promoting competition by reducing switching costs may not be effective. These papers, however, ignore search frictions and assume that consumers have perfect information about market prices. This assumption is reasonable in markets where prices are posted rather than individually negotiated. In general, with posted prices, forward-looking firms and consumers need to understand how their current actions affect the evolution of market shares, which influence their payoffs in future periods. In contrast, if prices are individually negotiated, we can treat each consumer as an unconnected market where firm pricing is independent of their market shares. This simplifies the dynamic problem but also brings forth new challenges.
In our setting, we only observe the final contract rates, rather than all the quotes that borrowers obtain. We therefore need to explicitly model how the observed price distributions are generated and how they are affected by search and switching frictions. Our approach is to approximate price negotiation as an English procurement auction, where lenders gradually lower their quotes to bid for a borrower. This assumption captures in a tractable way the important market features that (i) borrowers use the best offer in hand to extract better quotes, and (ii) lenders are willing to accept profitable counteroffers. The auction setting provides a clear interpretation of the final price, which is associated with the second-order statistic of lenders' reservation values. Other studies applying auction-like models to approximate price negotiation include Woodward and Hall (2012), Rosenbaum (2013), Allen, Clark, and Houde (2019), Salz (2022), Slattery (2019), and Cuesta and Sepulveda (2021).
There is now a growing literature that investigates both search and switching frictions in a unified framework. However, we are not aware of any studies exploring the interaction between these two frictions. Wilson (2012) points out that models taking into account only one type of market friction can generate biased estimates when both frictions exist. He uses a static framework and finds that in theory, search costs tend to be more harmful to consumers than switching costs. Honka (2014) quantifies search and switching costs in the U.S. auto insurance markets using information on consumers' consideration sets, purchase prices, and switching behavior. These studies do not account for supply-side responses when calculating the welfare effects due to changes in market frictions. Braido and Ledo (2018) build a parsimonious model of dynamic pricing competition with search and switching frictions to rationalize the coexistence of zero and positive fees in the Brazilian auto insurance brokerage market. In their model, firms offer individualized prices but do not allow negotiation. This does not fit into the setting of negotiated-price markets, where the key feature is that customers use their current best offers to negotiate for better prices.
Finally, our paper is related to studies on the relationship between competition and refinancing, in particular analyses on the effectiveness of the U.S. Home Affordable Refinancing Program (HARP) and Home Affordable Modification Program (HAMP). Research has largely attributed the ineffectiveness of these policies to intermediation frictions and poor market design (see Amromin and Kearns (2014), Agarwal et al. (2017), Abel and Fuster (2021), Agarwal et al. (2023a), Fuster and Willen (2024), and Piskorski and Seru (2018)). We focus on the role of search and switching costs as well as the resulting pricing dynamics. Closely related to our stress-test counterfactual, Agarwal et al. (2023a) show that HARP unintentionally strengthened the incumbency advantage in mortgage refinancing. When this advantage was removed, the authors document an increase in refinancing and better rates.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIintroduces institutional details and the data. SectionIIdescribes the model and characterizes the equilibrium. SectionIIIspecifies our empirical framework. SectionIVpresents the estimation results. SectionVquantifies the market frictions. SectionVIpresents our policy applications. Finally, SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Institutional Details and Data:
The Canadian mortgage market is dominated by a small number of large players, including six national banks (Bank of Montreal, Bank of Nova Scotia, Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce, National Bank of Canada, Royal Bank of Canada, and Toronto-Dominion Bank), one regional cooperative network (Desjardins in Quebec), one provincially owned financial institution (ATB Financial in Alberta), two banks operating primarily in specific provinces (Laurentian Bank of Canada and HSBC Bank Canada), and two mortgage finance companies operating nationally (MCAP and First National). Together these lenders originate more than 85% of the residential mortgages in Canada. For brevity, we refer to these major lenders as the “big 12.” Other lenders include local credit unions and private lenders. In addition, mortgage brokers can serve as intermediaries.
Posted mortgage rates are set weekly and nationally. Lenders post their mortgage rates across different maturities, and these are common across all local markets. Website aggregators then advertise these rates along with a host of other lender rates. The Bank of Canada uses these rates to construct a benchmark rate that is used as part of the government's macroprudential policy toolkit. Nonbroker mortgage applications are done at the branch level and not electronically. Normally, borrowers visit a few banks in a local market and negotiate with branch managers to receive discounts off the posted rate. Banks compete with rival banks in prices, but branches of the same bank do not compete against each other. Finally, the majority of insured mortgages (i.e., those mortgages with loan-to-value [LTV] ratios greater than 80% at origination) are securitized.
In Canada, a typical newly originated mortgage amortizes in 25 years. The loan term, however, is much shorter, between 1 and 10 years, during which time the interest rate is either fixed or variable. The majority of home buyers take out five-year FRMs. Hence, every five years they are forced to renew and obtain a new interest rate for the remaining balance.5Mortgage markets in many countries (e.g., Netherlands, Switzerland, United Kingdom) are similar: borrowers periodically renew short-term FRMs over a longer amortization period. This feature makes studying banks' pricing strategies substantially easier than the U.S. market, where borrowers sign long-term contracts and have an option to refinance when it is advantageous to do so. Chen, Michaux, and Roussanov (2018), for example, document strong countercyclical mortgage refinancing activity associated with equity extraction. The refinancing decision is therefore endogenous. This substantially complicates borrowers' search and switch decisions as well as lenders' pricing strategies. Mortgage renewal in Canada, in contrast, is almost entirely exogenous and depends on the date of origination. We take advantage of the deterministic timing for repeated interactions to gain insights into the dynamic pricing strategies of lenders.
How do renewals work in Canada? Toward the end of a borrower's five-year contract, the incumbent lender sends the borrower a notice by mail about the upcoming renewal and offers a rate.6The borrower can accept the quote or search for better offers from rival banks. A clear advantage for the incumbent is that borrowers face nontrivial switching costs. In addition, unlike in posted-price markets, since pricing is opaque, borrowers need to visit rival banks and negotiate to obtain meaningful offers. The home bank enjoys a first-mover advantage by offering an initial quote that might prevent the borrower from conducting a search.
Our main data set comes from TransUnion, a credit bureau company that collects information on credit products for the Canadian population. The data set contains anonymized information on borrowers' characteristics: age, credit score, nonmortgage debt, and address at the forward sortation area (FSA) level.7We also observe mortgage contract information, including the lender's identity, loan size, funding date, monthly payment, outstanding balance, and an indicator for mortgage insurance. Crucially, we use borrowers' credit history to identify their switching activities. We define a new borrower's home bank by the premortgage relationships built on other credit products. Finally, we include data on financial inquiries.
In addition to monthly credit bureau data, we access an anonymized contract-level administrative data set, which offers information on mortgages provided by federally regulated lenders. We match individuals at the loan-account level. This data set is similar to that used in Allen, Clark, and Houde (2019).8From this data set, we get the mortgage rate and details such as borrower income, broker usage, house value, loan term, amortization, loan-to-value ratio, and total debt-servicing ratio. We also supplement the data set with 2016 FSA-level demographic information such as population and average household income. Finally, we include the quarterly FSA-level house price index generated by Teranet.
We construct a cross-sectional sample of new borrowers and first-time renewers who negotiated their interest rates within the period from January 2014 to July 2019. After dropping borrowers with a home equity line of credit or multiple liens, the full sample contains 771,101 contracts. Columns (1)–(3) in TableA1provide summary statistics for this sample. The switching rate at renewal is 9.55%. From this sample, we further restrict our analysis to FRM-5Y contracts with government-backed insurance (50.1% of the sample). FRM-5Y mortgages account for 56% of all mortgages and 59% of all insured mortgages. We also include a number of other sample restrictions: (i) exclude broker transactions (30%), (ii) exclude movers (2%), and (iii) exclude contracts in TransUnion for which we do not have accurate information in our administrative database (39%). This results in an intermediate sample with 48,951 purchase, 36,150 loyal renewal, and 1,650 switch renewal contracts. Columns (4)–(6) of TableA1provide summary statistics for this sample. The switching rate at renewal in this sample is 4.4%. The lower switching rate in the intermediate sample is because, for switch renewals, it is harder to find a match in the administrative data set due to changes in contract information. We therefore randomly drop purchases and loyal renewers from the intermediate sample in order to match the switching probability in the full sample. In the end, we have 17,277 purchase, 15,627 loyal renewal, and 1,650 switch renewal contracts. TableIpresents summary statistics for the final sample, which are close to those for the intermediate sample (TableA1). In addition, TableA2shows the reduced-form results from regressing mortgage rates on borrower and contract characteristics using the full sample and our final sample. The coefficients are very similar. The sample fit is greater in the restricted sample because we are focusing on a relatively homogeneous product.
A few words on some of the sample restrictions. First, we focus on insured mortgages only. Mortgage insurance is mandatory for all mortgages with an LTV ratio above 80% at origination. Importantly, mortgage insurance is paid upfront for the life of the mortgage—this is in contrast to the United States, where a borrower can stop paying for insurance once the LTV ratio falls below 80%. This feature greatly simplifies borrower and lender decisions at renewal. All lenders are competing for insured mortgages, that is, there is no default risk.
Second, we exclude mortgages intermediated by brokers. Adding brokers to our dynamic (negotiated) pricing game substantially complicates the analysis, which needs to account for broker-lender relationships, lenders' participation and pricing in broker channels, brokers' search and pricing strategies, etc. Not only would banks and consumers be forward-looking, but so would brokers. We do not have sufficient data on broker availability, broker-lender relationships, and broker compensation to do justice to this question. In TableA3, we show strong persistence in borrower choices of origination channels, suggesting that excluding the broker channel should have little impact on our model estimates for branch-based mortgages. See Robles-Garcia (2019) for a detailed analysis of mortgage brokers in the U.K. market.
In this subsection, we present descriptive evidence that motivates our structural model. In SectionII, we build a model that captures and explains these salient market features. TableIpresents summary statistics for borrowers' characteristics and contract information. TableIIreports regression results that describe the correlations between negotiated contract rates and borrower characteristics. We present five key features that characterize the pricing pattern and shopping behavior in the Canadian mortgage market.
Feature 1: Mortgage rates are determined via negotiation. Borrowers rarely pay mortgage rates posted by lenders. They negotiate to obtain individual discounts. Figure1plots residual rate dispersion, after controlling for a rich set of observables (including lender fixed effects), for the three sets of contracts in our data: originations (purchases), loyal renewals, and switch renewals. We can see that there remains substantial variation.
Feature 2: Borrowers shop around for lower interest rates, taking into account the cost of obtaining quotes and switching. Mortgage products offered by different lenders are fairly homogeneous. According to CMHC (2018), the top reason for a borrower to choose a specific lender is a better interest rate. Other than rates, borrowers value convenience and trust in existing relationships. Convenience and trust in preexisting relationships is one reason that we assume in SectionIIthat borrowers' first contact is an existing lender.
Next, features 3 and 4 look at switching and search frictions, respectively. In markets with negotiated prices and repeated interactions, it is not surprising that nontrivial search and switching frictions coexist. It is costly to obtain quotes due to the time-consuming application and negotiation process. After obtaining quotes, it is also costly to switch lenders—there could be monetary costs such as mortgage transfer fees and nonmonetary costs like brand loyalty and preference for one-stop banking.
Feature 3: Switching frictions. Renewers rarely switch even though switchers tend to have better interest rates. TableIshows that only 9.55% of renewers switch banks.9This is despite the fact that loyal renewers pay a loyalty premium of 8.5 bps. In the linear probability regression for switching decisions reported in TableII, we find that switching renewals are associated with younger borrowers, borrowers with lower credit scores, a higher outstanding balance, a longer amortization period, and more local lenders.
Feature 4: Search frictions. Borrowers with access to more lenders receive on average better rates. However, most borrowers obtain quotes only from a subset of lenders available in their local markets.10TableIshows that the average number of available lenders in local markets is close to seven. In contrast, the average number of quotes reported in Allen, Clark, and Houde (2014) is under three, suggesting the presence of search frictions.11Similarly, CMHC (2018) finds that borrowers contact on average 3.1 lenders at origination and only 2.5 lenders at renewal. The fact that renewers search less is consistent with the rate regression results presented in TableII: the number of lenders in local markets is associated with lower rates, but the effect is weaker at renewal.
Feature 5: An “invest-and-harvest” pricing pattern. Borrowers renewing with their home bank tend to pay higher interest rates than new borrowers and those renewers who switch to rival lenders. This can be seen from the summary statistics in TableIas well as the regression estimates in TableII.
In addition to the key features of our restricted sample outlined above, there are features in the full sample that provide further supporting evidence for the dynamic pricing pattern in the Canadian mortgage market (see TableA2). Relative to the baseline category (four- to five-year FRM) and controlling for the term-specific bond rates, short-term mortgages have low rates, while longer-term mortgages have higher rates. The observed mortgage rate differences therefore can be attributed in part to how lenders discount future incumbency advantages. For short-term mortgages, borrowers are up for renewal in the near future, and hence, the incumbency advantage in the next period is discounted less heavily—lenders compete more aggressively with lower rates. In addition, relative to the baseline category (Amortization≤$\le$5 year), mortgages with longer amortization have lower rates. This can be explained by a game-ending effect: in the last period, when borrowers are about to pay off their mortgage, lenders have no investment incentive, and thus rates are higher.
We interpret the dynamic pricing pattern in the data and loyalty premium (difference in rates paid by switchers and nonswitchers) as a direct result of the incumbency advantage induced by the switching and search frictions highlighted in Features 3 and 4. However, there might be competing explanations. For example, the relationship-lending literature (see Sharpe (1990), Rajan (1992), and Von Thadden (2004)) suggests that if incumbent lenders have better information about borrowers'quality, it can create a hold-up problem that allows home banks to retain their customers more easily and extract informational rents. This is not supported by the data. Intuitively, borrowers with better credit scores are less likely to be locked-in due to an informational advantage—their credit history is well documented. Therefore, if the loyalty premium captures the informational rent enjoyed by incumbent lenders, it should be smaller for high-credit-score borrowers. We find the opposite. Columns (4) and (5) of TableIIshow that borrowers with a high credit score are less likely to switch, and on average pay a larger loyalty premium, suggesting that asymmetric information is unlikely to be the main concern for lenders. This is perhaps not surprising given that we focus on insured mortgages and all lenders have access to the same credit bureau information.
Another feature of the data consistent with an invest-and-harvest pricing pattern is that lenders compete more aggressively for borrowers who are less likely to switch in the future. From the discussion above, we know that borrowers with higher credit scores are less likely to switch and on average pay a larger loyalty premium, while column (1) in TableIIshows that rates are decreasing in credit scores. Another example would be amortization. Column (5) of TableIIshows that borrowers with longer amortization are more likely to switch at renewal, possibly due to greater potential savings in interest costs from rate reductions. This result is consistent with Fisher et al. (2024): borrowers in the United Kingdom are more likely to actively refinance for better rates when the remaining term to maturity is longer. Meanwhile, TableIIand TableA2show that for mortgages with amortization longer than five years, even after controlling for switching activities, interest rates are increasing in amortization. This finding differs from Fisher et al. (2024), where by virtue of posted prices, borrowers pay similar rates conditional on refinancing.
The correlations presented above appear to line up with our intuition about lender pricing in the presence of market frictions. However, there could be other explanations. For example, borrowers with higher credit scores tend to have better rates because bank lending costs are lower as less verification is needed. In general, we need a structural model to account for how borrower/contract characteristics separately affect the costs and benefits of search/switch, lending costs, and lenders' investment incentives, which together determine the observed mortgage rates and switching decisions.
article.II. Model:
Consider a borroweri$i$searching for a mortgage contract with interest rate fixed form$m$years that amortizes inT×m$T \times m$years. We model this as aT$T$-period game. Each period can be further broken down into two stages: an initial quote stage in which the borrower receives a quote from her home bank and decides to accept or search for more quotes, and a negotiation stage in which the borrower negotiates with multiple lenders in her choice set if she rejects the home-bank offer. Since prices are individualized, we treat each borrower as an independent market. For brevity, we omit the borrower's indexi$i$and add it back in SectionIIIto emphasize borrower heterogeneity.
Borrower preferences. In each periodt=1,2,…,T$t=1,2,\ldots,T$, the borrower is attached to a home bankht$h^t$. Int=1$t=1$, the home bank is the lender that had provided the borrower with some product prior to the mortgage. Int>1$t>1$, the home bank is the lender providing the mortgage in the previous period.
In each periodt$t$, we divide the price-generating process into two stages. In the initial quote stage, the home bank offers a free quote. The borrower can accept the offer (end of the game in periodt$t$) or search for more quotes. Given the number of available banks in the local market,Nt$N^t$, the borrower decides the number of banks to be included in the choice set,nt$n^t$, and commits to take the best offer. At this point, the borrower has met with all lenders within the choice set and is ready to negotiate. In this stage, the negotiation process is approximated by an English auction: the borrower obtains quotes from all lenders in the choice set and uses the best offer in hand to negotiate for even better quotes. This process goes on until the borrower obtains an offer that no other lender is willing to beat. The winning lender provides the highest expected utility and becomes the borrower's home bank in the next period. The auction setting provides a clear interpretation of the final price, which is associated with the second-order statistic of lenders' reservation values.
The borrower and lenders learn about borrower preferences and lending costs in sequence. In the initial quote stage, the home bank,ht$h^t$, notices that the borrower is looking for a new mortgage (t=1$t=1$) or renewing her remaining balance (t>1$t>1$). The state variables commonly observed by both parties are the home-bank identityht$h^t$, the number of locally available lendersNt$N^t$, the common cost realizationct∼F(·)$c^t \sim F(\cdot)$, and the switching costλt$\lambda ^t$. The search cost distributionH(·)$H(\cdot)$and the idiosyncratic cost distributionG(·)$G(\cdot)$are also common knowledge, but the search cost realizationκt$\kappa ^t$is the borrower's private information. For simplicity, assume thatNt$N^t$andλt$\lambda ^t$do not vary over time.16Given the state vector(ht,N,ct,λ,κt)$(h^t,N,c^t,\lambda,\kappa ^t)$in the initial quote stage, among which(ht,N,ct,λ)$(h^t,N,c^t,\lambda)$are commonly observed whileκt$\kappa ^t$is private to the borrower, the home bank chooses a pricep0t$p^t_0$and the borrower decidesnt$n^t$.
In the negotiation stage, each lender in the choice set draws an IID idiosyncratic cost shockωjt$\omega ^t_j$fromG(·)$G(\cdot)$. The distribution is commonly known, but the cost realization is private information. Denote the full state vector in periodt$t$byst=(ht,N,ct,λ,κt,ωt)$s^t=(h^t,N,c^t,\lambda,\kappa ^t,\bm{\omega }^t)$, whereωt$\bm{\omega }^t$is the vector of idiosyncratic cost draws. At this point, lenderj$j$chooses the quote to offerpjt$p^t_j$and the borrower determines the winner in the English auction (wt$w^t$).
In what follows, we first characterize the equilibrium bidding strategies and equilibrium pricing functions in the negotiation stage conditional on the borrower's chosen choice set. We then solve the borrower's problem of choice-set formation and the home bank's problem of optimal initial-quote offering.
In each periodt$t$, conditional onnt$n^t$, we solve for the equilibrium pricing functions. Ifnt=1$n^t = 1$, the borrower is satisfied with her home bank's initial offer and does not enter into the negotiation stage. The equilibrium price ispt∗=p0t$p^{t*} = p^t_0$.
Ifnt≥2$n^t \ge 2$, lenders compete in expected utility via a descending English procurement auction.17This captures two important features in the negotiation process: (1) borrowers use the best offer in hand to extract better quotes, and (2) lenders are willing to lower their offers to win as long as they expect positive profits. In the end, the highest surplus lender wins at a price that just beats the second best option for the borrower.
Given the home bank's offerp0t$p_0^t$and the search cost realizationκt$\kappa ^t$, the borrower's trade-off is between acceptingp0t$p_0^t$or paying(nt−1)κt$(n^t-1)\kappa ^t$to obtain the expected winning priceE[pt∗(st)|nt]$E[p^{t*}(s^t)|n^t]$, where the expectation is taken with respect to the idiosyncratic cost shocks drawn by thent$n^t$lenders in the choice set.20
We can now summarize the results obtained from the initial quote stage and the negotiation stage. Given the choice setnt$n^t$and the state vectorst$s^t$, the equilibrium price from the auctionpt∗$p^{t*}$is described in equation (5). Knowing the payoff from acceptingp0t$p_0^t$and the expected payoff from searchingnt$n^t$lenders, the borrower choosesnt$n^t$optimally to solve the search problem (8). Anticipating the search intensity (equation (10)) and the corresponding auction outcome, the home bank chooses the optimal initial quotep0t∗$p_{0}^{t*}$to maximize its expected profit.
Intuitively, the investment incentive is always increasing in the switching cost. It tends to be smaller, however, if lenders expect search costs to be small, because retaining the borrower becomes less likely in the next period. Other things being equal, asG(·)$G(\cdot)$gets more dispersed, the expected marginal saving from searching an extra bank increases, the borrower obtains more quotes, and the home bank finds it harder to retain the borrower, and henceVt$V^{t}$tends to be smaller. Its relationship withN$N$is more subtle. If search costs are expected to be low on average, higherN$N$implies more quotes and more competition in the next period, and henceVt$V^{t}$should be lower. However, if search costs are very high on average, the borrower would not obtain more quotes even thoughN$N$increases. In such case,Lt$L^t$decreases because the chance of being selected in the next period gets smaller, and therefore,Vt$V^{t}$could even increase inN$N$.
article.III. Empirical Specification:
For every borrower in the data, we observe (i) their characteristics (xit$\bm{x_i^t}$), (ii) the number of available lenders (Ni$N_i$), (iii) the home bank's identity, the chosen lender's identity, and hence the switching decision, and (iv) the final contract price. SectionIIdescribes how the observed equilibrium prices and switching behavior can be generated from our model primitives: (i) common cost distribution, (ii) idiosyncratic cost distribution, (iii) search cost distribution, (iv) switching cost, and (v) lenders' discount factor.22We wish to recover the model primitives from the observables.
In theInternet Appendix,23we present anonparametric thin-setidentification argument (see Khan and Tamer (2010)), assuming an ideal data set where we have an instrumental variable for the switching cost. Below, we specify and estimate a parametric version of the model. This allows us to incorporate the observable heterogeneity across borrowers and leverage variation in the full data rather than just information in the thin set used in the nonparametric identification proof.
Consider borroweri$i$in a market withNi$N_i$available lenders who is looking to originate or renew a mortgage with loan size $Mit$M_i^t$. We model lenders' common cost for providing the mortgage over the five-year term asMitcit$M_i^t c_i^t$, which naturally depends on the outstanding loan amount. The per-unit common costcit$c_i^t$is assumed to be drawn from a Normal distributionN(xitβ,σc2)$N(\bm{x_i^t \beta }, \sigma _c^2)$. The vectorxit$\bm{x_i^t}$includes borrower-specific observable characteristics such as outstanding loan amount, credit score, and amortization, as well as market characteristics such as the five-year bond rate, FSA-level income, housing transaction volume and price, and year and region fixed effects.24The loan sizeMit$M_i^t$is normalized so that the per-unit common cost measures the cost of a $100,000 mortgage.
In period 1 (origination), borroweri$i$’s mortgage loan size (origination amount) isMi1$M_i^1$. In period 2 (renewal), the outstanding amount isMi2<Mi1$M_i^2<M_i^1$since the borrower has partially paid down the mortgage. For each periodt$t$, we model the idiosyncratic cost for lenderj$j$in the negotiation stage asMitωi,j$M_i^t\omega _{i,j}$, whereωi,j$\omega _{i,j}$is drawn IID from a Type-1 extreme value distributionT1EV(γσω,σω)${\rm T1EV}(\gamma \sigma _{\omega },\sigma _{\omega })$.25This specification implies that as the outstanding amount decreases, (i) borrowers are less likely to get a great match with rival lenders, and (ii) borrowers are less like to search and/or switch because there is less to gain. The outstanding amount therefore is a payoff-relevant state variable. To calculate their optimal pricing strategies, lenders need to form beliefs regarding the outstanding amount in periodt+1$t+1$. We do not model the complicated process of how lenders adjust their beliefs as they observe rivals' bids.26Instead, we assume that in equilibrium: (i) all lenders believe that the outstanding amount evolves according to the winning price and the remaining amortization period, and (ii) no lender has an incentive to beat the winning price.
Given our parametric assumptions, we can calculate the likelihood contribution of a borrower switching to a rival lender (or staying with the home bank) and paying a certain price. The details are described in theInternet Appendix. We estimate the vector of parameters(σc,σω,δ,α,β,λ)$(\sigma _c,\sigma _{\omega },\delta,\bm{\alpha },\bm{\beta },\bm{\lambda })$that maximizes the likelihood function.
Before proceeding to the estimation results, we briefly describe the sources of variation in the data that allow us to pin down the parametric model. The model is nonlinear, and hence all of the information from the data contributes to estimating each parameter. However, some features of the data are more relevant for identifying certain parameters.
The key identification challenge is to separate out search and switching frictions without observing search. These two frictions have similar effects on many of the equilibrium outcomes, both leading to less switching, a larger loyalty premium, and higher investment incentives. If we directly observed the number of quotes obtained by borrowers, distinguishing between the market frictions would be straightforward. Despite the lack of information on search, however, we show that identification is possible given the observed correlation between switching probability and the number of lenders across local markets.
First, note that only search costs affect the pass-through of number of lenders (N$N$) to number of quotes (n$n$). This pass-through is determined by the fraction of borrowers choosing to contact all available lenders (i.e.,n=N$n=N$). As search costs decrease,Pr(n=N)$Pr(n=N)$increases, and hence the degree of pass-through becomes stronger, approaching one-to-one when quotes are free. In contrast, larger switching costs do not prevent borrowers from gathering more quotes: regardless of how costly it is to switch, borrowers can always use competitive offers from rival lenders as leverage to negotiate for better rates from their home bank. Second, borrowers obtaining more quotes are more likely to find a better offer from rival banks and hence are more likely to switch. As a result, the effect ofN$N$on the switching probability is stronger when search costs are lower, while it is barely affected by switching costs.27Moreover, if we observe that some borrower characteristics reduce the competitive effect ofN$N$on the switching probability, these variables should be positively associated with search costs. For example, whenN$N$increases, if the switching probability for high-credit-score borrowers increases by less than for borrowers with low credit scores, then we can infer that high credit scores are associated with high search costs. In short, the incumbency advantage (switching probability and loyalty premium) informs us about the possible search/switching cost combinations, and the estimated correlation between the switching probability and the number of lenders separately identifies search and switching costs. In addition, information on how borrower characteristics affect this correlation indicates how search costs are determined. The remaining association between borrower characteristics and switching probability, not explained by search frictions, informs us about how switching costs are determined.
We now turn to the other parameters. The residualized price dispersion is explained by randomness in the common cost, idiosyncratic cost, and search cost, but only the first two depend on the outstanding amount. In addition,σω$\sigma _{\omega }$affects the expected benefit from searching, the switching probability, the investment incentive, and hence the pricing, butσc$\sigma _{c}$does not. We can therefore separately pin downσω$\sigma _{\omega }$andσc$\sigma _{c}$. Lastly, for mortgages with the same outstanding balance, a longer amortization period implies a larger outstanding amount at renewal. As a result, lenders' investment incentives and current-period pricing are affected by the number of periods before the mortgage is fully paid, consistent with the descriptive evidence in TableA2. This relationship between price and amortization helps pin down the discount factor,δ$\delta$.
article.IV. Estimation Results:
TableIIIdisplays the maximum likelihood estimates from both our benchmark dynamic model and a static model that restricts lenders' discount factorδ=0$\delta =0$. In the dynamic model, we estimate a lender discount factor of 0.71 over a five-year span, which translates into an annual discount factor of 0.93. The likelihood ratio test rejects the static model at the 0.1% significance level.
The loan size is normalized such that the estimated parameters, measured in $1,000, describe how the interest cost of a $100,000 mortgage is determined by the observable characteristics and random shocks. For example, in the first row,σc=0.8542$\sigma _c = 0.8542$implies that the standard deviation of the common cost for lending $100,000 over a five-year term is $854.2. We next discuss the economic magnitude of our estimates.
Lending costs. The standard deviation of the idiosyncratic cost distribution is $232.9, which is about one-fourth of the standard deviation of the common cost shock.28This means that most of the unexplained price variation should be attributed to unobserved borrower heterogeneity rather than idiosyncratic differences across banks.
The dispersion in the idiosyncratic cost distribution is key for understanding borrowers' search decisions. When banks' idiosyncratic costs vary a lot, borrowers are more likely to find banks with a low enough price to switch to, and hence are more likely to search. Figure2shows a median borrower's expected marginal benefit of adding an extra bank to the choice set at origination and renewal. The expected marginal benefit decreases as the choice set gets larger. The expected marginal benefit is larger for new borrowers atn≥2$n \ge 2$because they have a larger outstanding balance and hence more dispersed idiosyncratic cost shocks. However, atn=1$n=1$, the opposite is true: the benefit of obtaining two competing quotes rather than accepting the home bank's initial offer is smaller for new borrowers. Home banks understand that it is harder to retain new borrowers since they tend to obtain more quotes and have lower switching costs (shown in TableIV). Therefore, new borrowers tend to receive more appealing initial offers and there is less to gain from searching.
Turning to the mean of the common cost component, the coefficient estimates all have intuitive interpretations. The mean common cost is decreasing in credit score and increasing in the bond rate. On average, mortgages with a higher outstanding balance or shorter amortization cost less per unit. The lending costs are on average lower in markets with higher incomes, higher house prices, and greater volume of housing transactions.
Search costs. Since we do not observe search cost realizations or search decisions, we use a simulated sample to help understand the estimated search cost distribution. We simulate 200,000 contracts by sampling borrowers' observable characteristics from the empirical distribution and drawing search and lending cost shocks from the estimated distributions. We then solve for equilibrium outcomes and summarize the variables of interest in TableIV. See SectionIV.Bfor more details about the simulation process.
On average, borrowers search for fewer quotes (3) than the number of available lenders (7), due to the relatively small dispersion in the estimated idiosyncratic cost distribution. New borrowers tend to search more than renewers: 3.2 versus 2.8 quotes. This is consistent with CMHC (2018), which reports 3.1 and 2.5 quotes for new borrowers and all renewers, respectively.29Searchers, on average, have much lower per-bank search costs than do non-searchers: $162 versus $789. On average they obtain 4.2 quotes, one of which is from the home bank.30Figure3shows how average search costs vary with credit score, FSA income, and age. Credit scores and FSA income play major roles in shaping search costs. This is intuitive—they are both positively correlated with borrower income, and therefore, a good proxy for individuals' opportunity costs of the time-consuming search and negotiation process. In addition, search costs are on average decreasing in age, possibly due to experience.
Switching costs. From TableIV, we see that, on average, renewers face much higher switching costs than new borrowers: $829 versus $462. This is reasonable given the extra fees and inconveniences incurred from transferring mortgages across lenders relative to, say, a credit card.31For a mortgage with median origination amount ($212,000), Figure3shows the variation in renewers' switching costs by credit score, FSA income, and age. Switching costs are increasing in age, and to a lesser extent increasing in credit score.
Relationship between frictions, investment incentives, and rates. Given the structural model estimates, we can calculate the switching cost, the expected search cost, the expected common lending cost, and the investment incentive for each borrower. With these in hand, we can then explore the mechanisms developed in SectionII: (i) the search/switching costs create an incumbency advantage that allows home banks to easily capture their customers with relatively high rates; (ii) anticipating the future incumbency advantage, lenders have more incentive to invest in borrowers with higher search/switching costs in future periods; and (iii) higher investment incentives lead to more competitive rates in the current period. TableVpresents the results. Column (1) shows that, in the data, borrowers with higher estimated search/switching costs are less likely to switch at renewal. Not surprisingly, the empirical switching probability does not depend on the estimated lending cost, but is increasing in the dispersion of the idiosyncratic cost shock (σωM$\sigma _{\omega }M$).32In column (2), we find that the estimated investment incentive is increasing in search and switching costs, while it is decreasing inσωM$\sigma _{\omega }M$and the number of lenders since they make borrowers more likely to switch. We also find an interaction effect: the positive relationship between the investment incentive and switching cost is stronger when search cost is high. Finally, column (3) shows that after controlling for the estimated lending costs, the number of lenders, and the current-period search/switching costs, borrowers with higher estimated investment incentives receive lower rates on average.
Distinction between search costs and switching costs. From TableV, we observe that search and switching costs influence lender competition and pricing in similar ways—both leading to less switching, stronger investment incentives, and higher rates. This raises the question of how these frictions can be separately identified. In TableVI, we show that the key difference between search and switching costs lies in how they affect borrowers' search intensity and hence in how they affect the correlation between switching probability and number of lenders, supporting the identification argument presented in SectionIII.
Specifically, we simulate 10,000 renewers with median observable characteristics for nine different markets structures, where the number of lenders varies fromN=2,3,4$N=2, 3, 4$,…,10$\ldots, 10$. For each market structure, we assign one-quarter of the observations with search and switching costs at (0%,0%), (0%,100%), (100%,0%), and (100%,100%) of the estimated levels, respectively. This simulated sample allows us to focus only on the variation in search cost, switching cost, and number of lenders. In column (1), we find that the pass-through from number of lenders (N$N$) to number of quotes (n$n$) equals 100% if there were no search or switching costs. The pass-through drops to 18% (1.00−0.82$1.00-0.82$) in the presence of search costs. In contrast, it is barely affected by switching costs. As a result, in column (2), search costs weaken the competitive effect ofN$N$on switching probability while switching costs do not. Therefore, the association between switching probability andN$N$observed in the data informs us about search costs. One may also wonder whether a similar argument can be applied to interest rates. Column (3), however, shows that both market frictions can weaken the competitive effect ofN$N$on rates and can increase the loyalty premium. It is therefore hard to distinguish between search and switching costs based simply on the observed correlation between rates andN$N$.
In this subsection, we analyze the goodness of fit of the benchmark model. If the model approximates the underlying data-generating process, it should fit the key moments observed in the data. In addition, given the extra complexity of including both market frictions in the model, we are interested in whether a search-cost-only model or a switching-cost-only model can fit the data. To shed light on these questions, we estimate restricted models where we have only one type of market friction. The likelihood ratio tests reject each of the single-friction models at the 0.1% significance level.33
TableVIIsummarizes the key variables of interest in the simulated samples. Not surprisingly, relative to the benchmark model, each of the single-cost models generates a higher estimate for the friction in question. Column (2) shows that, without search costs, borrowers contact all available lenders. This is inconsistent with the survey evidence provided by CMHC (2018), reporting 3.1 and 2.5 quotes for new borrowers and renewers, respectively. In contrast, the search-cost-only model in column (3) predicts fewer quotes than the survey numbers. Moreover, it predicts that 36.8% of borrowers switch at renewal, while the empirical switching probability is only 9.6%.35Consequently, it also underestimates the mean rate difference between new borrowers and renewers.
We can also examine which of the models can better match the observed correlation between switching probability and number of lenders—a key data moment that helps distinguish search and switching frictions. In Figure4, we first show the correlation between switching probability and number of lenders, as well as how it is affected by borrower characteristics, in a regression framework using the data sample. Specifically, we define an indicator variableI(high credit score)that equals one if the borrower has a credit score above the median score. We similarly define indicator variablesI(high income)andI(high age). We then regressI(Switch)on the number of lenders,I(high credit score),I(high income),I(high age), the interaction between these indicator variables and the number of lenders, along with other control variables, including amortization and loan size. We find that the number of lenders has a positive effect on switching probability. The effect is stronger when borrowers are older, but weaker when income is high and even weaker when credit score is high. As we show in TableVI, since the effect of the number of lenders on switching probability is dampened by search costs but not affected by switching costs, we can infer that older borrowers tend to have lower search costs while higher credit score and income are associated with higher search costs, all of which are consistent with our benchmark search-cost estimates and Figure3.36
Next, we examine whether our estimated models can reproduce the aforementioned data patterns. To do so, we simulate 1,000 samples of 34,554 borrowers using the benchmark dual-friction model, the switching-friction model, and the search-friction model.37For each of the simulated samples, we run the same switching decision regression as above. We are interested in whether the estimates from the simulated samples are close to those obtained from the data. Figure4shows that our benchmark model fits the data patterns well. As expected, the estimates from samples generated by the switching-friction-only model are always larger than those obtained from the data, while the search-friction-only model tends to produce a weaker correlation between switching probability and number of lenders.
In summary, comparing the single-friction models to the benchmark highlights the need to include both search and switching costs. Switching costs are needed to capture the low probability of switching at renewal, while search costs are needed to capture the fact that borrowers do not gather many quotes and hence the relatively weak correlation between the number of lenders and switching probability.
article.V. Quantifying Market Frictions:
Benchmark. TableVIIIsummarizes the equilibrium outcomes of different exercises predicted by both dynamic and static models. Column (1) shows the results simulated from our estimated benchmark with both market frictions. At both origination and renewal, relative to the dynamic benchmark, the static model predicts higher per-period profits for the winning bank, which equals the per-period total profit of all lenders, since losing banks earn zero. This is because the dynamic model rationalizes lower prices by either lower lending costs or higher investment incentives, while the static model ignores the latter component and hence underestimates the common component of lending costs. On the borrower side, the static model predicts on average a slightly lower, but not very different, total cost in each period than its dynamic counterpart. However, as we show below, because the dynamic model accounts for lenders' investment incentives while the static model does not, the welfare implications of the market frictions predicted by these two models can be quite different. Specifically, the static model always predicts larger consumer gains from eliminating search/switching costs.
Removal of switching costs. Column (2) describes an exercise where we eliminate switching costs. In the dynamic model, this increases switching. As a result, home banks suffer from the removal of switching costs while rival banks are better off. Since it becomes less attractive to be a home bank, lenders' investment incentive (V$V$) decreases, which explains why new borrowers face higher prices. Relative to the benchmark dynamic case, new borrowers face higher costs—an increase of $203 in interest costs and $75 (or 0.2%) in total costs. Renewers, in contrast, are better off, saving on average $54 in interest costs and $189 (or 0.6%) in total costs.38In contrast, the static model cannot capture changes in pricing resulting from changes in lenders' investment incentives, and therefore predicts greater savings in total costs for borrowers in both periods. The difference between the dynamic and static model predictions is further highlighted in Panel C of TableVIII, where we sum the total costs incurred at origination and subsequent renewal (assuming a consumer discount factorρ=1$\rho =1$). However, in qualitative terms, the comparison between the dynamic and static model predictions does not depend onρ$\rho$: the static model would predict larger consumer gains in all periods except for the last renewal.39
Removal of search costs. Column (3) reports the results from removing search costs. Since quotes are free, borrowers search all available lenders. The benefits of the extra quotes are significant. In our dynamic model, total costs for borrowers at origination fall by 1.1% and total costs at renewal fall by 1.2%. The static model predicts even higher total cost savings—2.1% at origination and 2.4% at renewal—because it ignores changes in lenders' investment incentives.
Removal of both frictions. Column (4) presents results from removing both switching costs and search costs. In this case, home banks do not have an incumbency advantage and lenders' investment incentives drop to zero. In our dynamic model, new borrowers' total costs decrease by 1.4%. Renewers benefit even more, paying 2.5% less than in the benchmark. The static model predicts total cost reductions of 3.0% and 4.6% at origination and at renewal, respectively.
Interaction of search and switching costs. The results above indicate that important interactions exist between the market frictions in thedynamicmodel: the effect of removing both frictions is not simply the sum of the effects of eliminating each friction individually. For example, eliminating both frictions leads to a 1.4% decrease in total cost at origination. This is larger in magnitude than the sum of the effects from individually eliminating switching costs (+0.2%) or search costs (−1.1%). This relationship also holds for renewal contracts:−2.5%<−0.6%−1.2%$-2.5\%<-0.6\%-1.2\%$. In other words, each market friction is less anticompetitive in the presence of the other type of friction. Interestingly, the interaction effect is less important in static models. The effect of removing both market frictions is a −3.0% (−4.6%) change in total costs at origination (renewal), which is very close to the sum of the effects from removing switching costs (−0.8%/−2.1%) and search costs (−2.1%/−2.4%) individually.
Figure5uses contour plots to show that equilibrium outcomes respond differently to changes in search and switching costs. In the plots, we have search costs on thex-axis and switching costs on they-axis. Each isocurve connects points for which the mean variable of interest has the same constant value. Note that at each point on the isocurve, the direction of the fastest increase is perpendicular to the isocurve. When the isocurves are closer together, the change in the variable of interest is faster as we change search/switching costs.
Panel A shows that total costs at origination are always increasing in search costs but could be decreasing in switching costs. At renewal, however, total costs are increasing in both costs, as shown in Panel B.40Both panels present the interaction between market frictions: when switching costs are low, the isocurves are closer together, meaning that total costs increase faster in search costs. This interaction effect is explained by Panels C and D: home-banks profit and hence lenders' investment incentives increase faster in search (switching) cost when switching (search) cost is higher. We can also see from Panel C that investment incentives tend to increase faster in switching costs than in search costs. Lastly, Panel E shows that borrowers' search intensity mainly depends on search costs rather than switching costs. Panel F suggests that switching frictions play an important role in lowering switching probability at renewal—it is hard to generate a switching rate lower than 20% with only search costs.
article.VI. Counterfactual Policy Experiments:
In this section, we use insights from our model to study two counterfactual policy experiments: (i) long-term contracting and (ii) mortgage stress testing. We highlight the importance of accounting for pricing dynamics in policy analysis.
With a typical amortization period of 25 years and a term of five years, borrowers face search and switching costs every time they negotiate their mortgages. A natural question is whether borrowers can benefit from longer-term contracts that reduce the frequency of renegotiation. In the counterfactual exercise, we focus on 25-year FRMs, which align the amortization period and term—similar to the U.S. market. Intuitively, when mortgage rates are fixed for the duration of the amortization period, borrowers have a strong incentive to search and switch at origination, but 25-year FRMs can still reduce total search and switching costs incurred throughout the amortization period. However, with these longer-term contracts, lenders may compete less aggressively for borrowers at origination, since they no longer enjoy a “harvest” period. Our structural model carefully accounts for the equilibrium responses of all players and allows us to compare borrowers' and lenders' payoffs under five-year FRMs versus longer-term contracts.
We consider two different scenarios: (i) only five-year FRMs are available and (ii) only 25-year FRMs are available. In the first scenario, we simulate 100,000 borrowers' new purchase contracts and all of their renewal contracts following the steps described in SectionV. We calculate the discounted sum of the interest costs, total costs, bank profits, and search/switching costs incurred over the entire 25-year amortization period. In the second scenario, mortgage rates are determined at origination and fixed for the amortization period. Loan officers therefore need to evaluate the profitability of borrowers over the next 25 years rather than over the next five years. The idiosyncratic match values should be more dispersed. To account for the greater uncertainty over a longer horizon, we scale up the variance of the idiosyncratic cost distribution for each borrower.41
TableIXpresents the comparison of mean equilibrium outcomes between five-year and 25-year mortgages obtained by simulating our dynamic model as well as a static version. The dynamic model predicts that on average all players are better off with 25-year FRMs. Borrowers enjoy a lower interest cost (−$213) and total cost (−$344), they search and switch more often at origination, but they incur lower search and switching costs throughout the amortization. Lenders, especially nonhome bank lenders, earn higher profits over 25 years. In sum, lenders obtain $423 more profits. This is in sharp contrast to the predictions from the static model. In the static model, lenders are predicted to earn $1,163 less in profits,42with home banks losing the most—their profits fall by $1,527 on average. The gains to consumers, in contrast, are larger than in the dynamic model—on average, interest costs are $1,824 lower and total costs $1,966 lower with long-term contracts.
Intuitively, the static model ignores changes in lenders' investment incentives when only 25-year FRMs are available. In dynamic models, because lenders no longer compete aggressively for future incumbency advantages, mortgage rates are on average higher in earlier years, which dampens the competitive effects predicted by a static model.
We next explore which groups of borrowers benefit most from long-term mortgages. On the one hand, the more dispersed distribution of idiosyncratic match values incentivizes borrowers with relatively high search/switching costs to obtain more quotes at origination. On the other hand, in a dynamic model, lenders' investment incentives for these borrowers decrease the most. For each borrower, we calculate the change in total financing costs. We then examine the relationship between total cost reductions and search/switching costs as well as some observable characteristics in a regression framework. TableXreports the results. Not surprisingly, in both the dynamic and the static models, borrowers with larger loans and access to more lenders enjoy greater savings. From column (1), the dynamic model suggests that total cost savings are decreasing in search costs but increasing in switching costs. We also find an interaction effect between the market frictions—for borrowers with higher switching costs, the negative relationship between total cost savings and search cost is even stronger. In stark contrast, the static-model results presented in column (3) show that total cost savings are increasing in both search costs and switching costs and their interaction term. This is because the static model ignores changes in lenders' investment incentives and hence predicts that those with higher search/switching costs benefit the most from long-term contracts. Such a difference between model predictions can also be seen from columns (2) and (4). Older borrowers, who tend to have higher switching costs but lower search costs, benefit more. The dynamic model suggests that borrowers with a lower credit score and lower income save more in total costs, while the static model predicts the opposite. For example, raising the credit score by 100lowersthe average total cost savings by $110 (or 32.0%) in our dynamic model, while itincreasesaverage total cost savings by $310 (or 15.8%) in the static counterpart.
Both the dynamic and the static models predict that on average borrowers enjoy lower total costs of mortgage financing when 25-year FRMs are available.43We can calculate the increase in funding costs (bond rate) required to make borrowers indifferent between five-year FRMs and 25-year FRMs: 1.79 bps in our dynamic model, and 10.14 bps in the static model. Both numbers are far smaller than the average difference (91 bps) in swap-adjusted five-year and 25-year bond rates during our sample period.44Our results therefore help explain why long-term mortgages are of little interest to borrowers in Canada.45
Since 2008, low interest rates in Canada stimulated housing market activities, with home buyers taking out larger mortgage loans than they could otherwise afford. Worried about large-scale default, the Department of Finance and the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI) introduced a series of stress tests to ensure that borrowers could afford their mortgage payments in the event of rising rates.46
The stress tests mainly target new borrowers at origination. However, the latest test, announced by OSFI in October 2017 and effective January 2018, also applies touninsuredborrowers at renewal should they switch to a different lender (but not if they stay with their home bank). OSFI emphasized that it does “not want borrowers who do not meet the increased underwriting standards to become the focus of price competition among lenders.”47Intuitively, there could be some unintended consequences: (i) unqualified renewers suffer from higher switching costs, (ii) home banks enjoy a larger incumbency advantage at renewal, and (iii) during the period when the test is announced but not yet implemented, anticipating the previous points, lenders would compete more aggressively for new borrowers who are expected to fail at renewal.
Given that the insured test rules are becoming increasingly similar to the uninsured ones, we are interested in understanding the potential impacts of the regulators further amending the stress test for insured borrowers to match that for uninsured borrowers. To estimate the counterfactual policy effects, we use insured new borrowers in our sample and assume that they are subject to the stress test at renewal but not at origination. We then investigate the test's impact on the purchase and subsequent renewal contracts.
At renewal, five years after mortgage origination, borrowers are older and the amortization period is shorter. Suppose that we know a borrowers' remaining balance and that all of the other observable characteristics stay the same. Using the borrowers' reported income and the qualifying rate (fixed by the regulator at 5.19%), we can calculate the maximum loan amount for which they could qualify.48
If a borrower's remaining balance at renewal is smaller than the qualified amount, the borrower can pass the stress test, in which case the equilibrium outcomes for this borrower are unaffected. However, if the remaining balance at renewal exceeds the qualified amount, the borrower fails the stress test and will need to pay down the excess balance if they want to switch lenders. We interpret this as an exogenous one-time increase in switching costs faced by unqualified renewers. Assuming that unqualified borrowers can borrow from private lenders at an annual interest rate of 10%,49the switching cost increase can then be approximated by the interest cost of borrowing the excess amount. To simulate the equilibrium outcomes for borrowers' purchase and subsequent renewal contracts, we follow steps similar to SubsectionVbut allow switching costs at renewal to be affected by the remaining balances.
TableXIsummarizes the predicted impacts of the counterfactual policy change on insured borrowers. Most renewers are unaffected—their outstanding balance is well below the qualified amount. On average, renewers are slightly less likely to switch and experience a 2.2 bps increase in interest rates. As a result, the anticipatory effect on an average new borrower is also small. The impact on borrowers who are expected to fail at renewal, however, is large. We find that about 10.2% of borrowers would fail the stress test at renewal. On average, their outstanding balance exceeds the maximum qualified amount by $31,677. These affected renewers need to incur more than three times their original switching costs to pass the test and switch to rival banks. As a result, home banks are able to retain about 97% of them and charge higher prices. On average, the unqualified renewers experience a 22 bps increase in interest rates and a 7.8% increase in total costs.50Finally, the incumbent lender's profit from an average unqualified renewer quadruples.
Turning to the origination period, anticipating high profits from an attached borrower who is expected to fail the test at renewal, lenders' investment incentive increases from $982 to $3,967 and the aggressive competition lowers the total costs by 4.6%.
Unintended by regulators, the anticipatory effect makes it easier for (riskier) borrowers—those who are expected to fail the stress test at renewal—to originate new mortgages. Intuitively, the lower rates incentivize borrowers with high GDS to take out larger loans. Moreover, some potential borrowers who would otherwise not enter the mortgage market might be attracted by the lower rates induced by the anticipatory effect. In our framework, every borrower demands a fixed loan size with no outside option. As a result, the model does not speak to the intensive and extensive adjustment in loan demand. We can nevertheless conduct back-of-the-envelope calculations to illustrate how the anticipatory effect might impact loan performance at renewal if we allow for loan-size adjustments.
For borrowers who are expected to fail the stress test at renewal, the anticipatory effect lowers their monthly payments at origination. Suppose that these borrowers are targeting a fixed GDS—they respond by borrowing more. Suppose that after five years, when borrowers renew their mortgages, they experience an unexpected bond rate spike of 200 bps, a possible scenario that motivates stress-testing. TableXIIshows, under different scenarios, the fraction of borrowers who would find it difficult to afford their housing expenses—their actual GDS exceeds certain thresholds. In Case 4, we assume that, given the anticipatory effects and lower rates at origination, borrowers take out larger loans so that their actual GDS at origination remains the same as in Case 1, where there is no stress test. The results from TableXIIsuggest that the unintended anticipatory effects could encourage borrowing and hence increase the share of borrowers in financial distress at renewal.
article.VII. Conclusion:
Using contract-level data for the Canadian mortgage market, we provide evidence of an “invest-and-harvest” pricing pattern. Lenders tend to offer relatively low interest rates to attract new borrowers and to poach rivals' existing customers, and they charge higher rates for loyal renewers. We build a dynamic model of price negotiation with search and switching frictions to capture key market features. We estimate the model and use it to investigate the effects of market frictions and the resulting dynamic competition on borrowers' and banks' payoffs. We show that by affecting lenders' investment incentives in dynamic pricing models, the interaction of market frictions can attenuate the anticompetitive effects of search and switching costs predicted by static models. Our framework is applicable to many other markets featuring repeated interaction, price negotiation, and search/switching frictions, with the caveat that extensions might be needed to account for certain institutional features, for example, endogenous refinancing decisions in the U.S. mortgage market and asymmetric information in small-business lending.
We study two counterfactual policy experiments through the lens of our model. The results highlight that it is important to account for pricing dynamics and the role of search and switching costs in policy analysis. We find that both borrowers and lenders benefit from long-term contracts if the funding costs remain largely unchanged. Heterogeneity exists across borrowers. Those with lower income, a lower credit score, larger loans, and more available lenders, as well as older borrowers, tend to benefit more. In the second counterfactual, we predict the unintended effects of applying mortgage stress tests to insured borrowers at renewal. We find that the policy change would distort lenders' pricing strategies by allowing them to charge higher rates at renewal but also compete more aggressively at origination.
